reason: Ep-002 verdict: MOLT — The cyberpunk aesthetic and Web Audio alarm are legitimate craft, but a Pomodoro timer without session tracking, break cycles, or productivity stats is a costume without a body — one molt focused on timer systems would double this score.

Ep-002 verdict: MOLT — The core split-pane editor works and runtime health is a solid 84 — a single molt adding scroll sync, a word count, and table support would double that completeness score overnight.

Ep-002 verdict: MOLT — The visual polish and wizard UX pattern are genuinely well-crafted (runtime health 82 proves it works), but it needs to be redirected toward installing or configuring something that actually exists within the RappterZoo ecosystem.

Ep-002 verdict: MOLT — The air-gapped optical messenger concept is genuinely novel and the runtime health is solid at 82, but it desperately needs actual camera capture on the receive end and a bigger feature surface to justify its existence.

Ep-002 verdict: MOLT — The mathematical core is sound and the visuals are inherently stunning — a molt adding color schemes, grid size options, speed controls, and an explanation panel would transform this from a proof-of-concept into a proper generative art showcase.

Ep-002 verdict: MOLT — The vaporwave aesthetic core and procedural city generation are legit — adding flight controls, a speed slider, and maybe a photo mode would transform this from passive flyover into an explorable experience.

Ep-002 verdict: MOLT — The core screen-recording capability is real and healthy at runtime, but it desperately needs recording settings, webcam repositioning, format options, and localStorage preferences to feel like a finished tool rather than a proof-of-concept.

Ep-002 verdict: MOLT — The concept is genuinely useful and the WebRTC foundation is solid — adding recording controls, a timer, format options, and download management would turn this tech demo into a legitimate creative tool.

Ep-002 verdict: MOLT — The geological erosion concept is evocative and the runtime health is solid at 77 — adding terrain layers, a proper save/export, and richer visual depth would transform this from a slider demo into something worth watching for actual millions of years.

title: Expansive Agents: Anticipating Business Needs
reason: Forensic audit (ep-010) found the filename presentation-slide-responsive-updated.html misled the Content Identity Engine through generations 2-5: v1 was a 1565-line AI Agent Swarm Simulator (65 canvas/game-loop refs), v2 collapsed it to a 302-line static business slide with 0 canvas refs, and v3-v5 polished the slide instead of the game. molt-log.json has no entries for gen 6 or gen 7 even though the file ballooned from ~10KB to 67KB and a game-shaped artifact returned. Score-91 hides the genre collapse; every future molt will repeat it as long as the filename says slide.
directive: Rename the file from presentation-slide-responsive-updated.html to expansive-agents-swarm.html. Update apps/manifest.json categories.games_puzzles.apps[].file to match. Add <meta name="rappterzoo:identity-mode" content="content-first"> in <head> so the Content Identity Engine reads the DOM (canvas tags, game loops, rappterzoo:type=game) instead of the filename string. Backfill apps/archive/presentation-slide-responsive-updated/molt-log.json with entries for generations 6 and 7 so the audit trail is continuous. Do not change gameplay code in this molt — this is an identity-repair pass only.
do_not: Do NOT add a new feature.
Do NOT regenerate or rewrite gameplay logic.
Do NOT touch v1-v5 archive files.
verification: After molt: filename matches manifest entry; rappterzoo:identity-mode meta tag present in head; molt-log.json has 7 sequential entries; score remains >=88.
diagnosis_one_line: The filename lies about what the file is, and every molt trusted the filename over the contents.
prescription_one_line: Rename the file to expansive-agents-swarm.html and add a rappterzoo:identity-mode=content-first meta tag so the Content Identity Engine reads the DOM, not the path.
